coredata.dc:title: Unveiling the effect of carbon dots on the TiO2-involved electron transport in perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Bingchen
Jia, Suping
Li, Yangyang
Zhang, Huinian
Hao, Caihong
Wang, Huiqi
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal-synthesized TiO2 was verified to effectively replace the commercial dyesol-TiO2 as the electron transport layer for promising perovskite devices. Still, the electron transport ability of this TiO2 is restricted by the surface defects. Herein, orange carbon dots prepared from p-phenylenediamine were employed as modifiers to regulate the TiO2-involved electron transport in perovskite solar cells. The results show that an appropriate amount of CDs can effectively reduce interface electron recombination, TiO2-CDs layers can improve the lattice growth of subsequent perovskite and result in efficient electron extraction from perovskite to TiO2. More importantly, the electron mobility of TiO2 increases from 3.00 × 10−5 to 4.13 × 10−5 cm2·V−1·s−1 due to the addition of CDs. In terms of photovoltaic performance, the photoelectric conversion efficiency reached 21.14% when TiO2-CDs were used as the mesoporous electron transfer layer, which is higher than that of TiO2-based PSCs (17.29%) under the same conditions. Meanwhile, the TiO2-CDs-based PSCs display negligible hysteresis, better stability and repeatability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs modifier
Interface electron transfer
Electron mobility
Perovskite solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles and pastes
Fabrication of perovskite solar cells (FTO/Compact TiO2/TiO2-CDs/FAMA/Spiro/Ag)
Characterization of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Science Foundation of North University of China ( XJJ201913 ), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi ( 2019L0597, 2020L0293 ), and the fund from Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials ( Wuhan University of Science and Technology ) ( WKDM202202 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) HRTEM images of TiO2-CDs. b) STEM images of the TiO2-CDs simultaneously acquired with EDS elemental maps for the (d) O, (e) C, and (f) Ti. c) EDS spectra of TiO2-CDs, with an estimated amount (in atomic %) of the respective elements.

a) The current density-voltage characteristics of perovskite solar cells prepared with different TiO2. b) J-V test with different scan directions for control TiO2 and TiO2-CDs based cells. c) The incident photon-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) spectra of cells with different TiO2.
(a) XRD results of FAMA perovskite samples, SEM results of FAMA perovskite samples deposited on (b) TiO2 and (c) 5:2 TiO2-CDs.
The steady-state PL spectrum (a), TRPL decays (primary and fitting results) (b) and EIS result (c) of perovskite deposited on control TiO2 and TiO2-CDs layers, respectively.
Dark current-voltage characteristic curves of PSCs with control TiO2 and modified TiO2.
The stability of unencapsulated PSCs based on different TiO2 in a certain environment.
Specific J-V test parameters of PSCs with different CDs.
The detailed parameters of the SCLC devices with different TiO2.

head.ce:title: Unveiling the effect of carbon dots on the TiO2-involved electron transport in perovskite solar cells

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Project administration, Writing − review & editing, Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal-synthesized TiO2 was verified to effectively replace the commercial dyesol-TiO2 as the electron transport layer for promising perovskite devices. Still, the electron transport ability of this TiO2 is restricted by the surface defects. Herein, orange carbon dots prepared from p-phenylenediamine were employed as modifiers to regulate the TiO2-involved electron transport in perovskite solar cells. The results show that an appropriate amount of CDs can effectively reduce interface electron recombination, TiO2-CDs layers can improve the lattice growth of subsequent perovskite and result in efficient electron extraction from perovskite to TiO2. More importantly, the electron mobility of TiO2 increases from 3.00 × 10−5 to 4.13 × 10−5 cm2·V−1·s−1 due to the addition of CDs. In terms of photovoltaic performance, the photoelectric conversion efficiency reached 21.14% when TiO2-CDs were used as the mesoporous electron transfer layer, which is higher than that of TiO2-based PSCs (17.29%) under the same conditions. Meanwhile, the TiO2-CDs-based PSCs display negligible hysteresis, better stability and repeatability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted extensive attention as the next generation of solar cells, arising from the facile solution technology process and their excellent performance, such as high charge carrier mobility, long diffusion length and high photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) [1–6]. As a new type of photovoltaic device, PSCs have developed dramatically with efficiency exceeding 25% in a few years [7–9]. It is found that the properties of the charge transfer layer are crucial factors in PSCs. The abatement of carrier recombination in the charge transfer layers and their involved interfaces can simultaneously meliorate charge extraction and transport [10,11].

Usually, TiO2 nanoparticles as the electron transport layer (ETL) are essential for promising perovskite devices. Inefficient charge transfer of TiO2 would induce the decrease of electron extraction from the perovskite active layer, leading to the decomposition of perovskite, meanwhile aggravating the charge recombination at the interface and in the mesoporous layer [12–14]. Therefore, TiO2 needs to be modified using suitable approaches to adapt to the current development of PSCs. Adding dopants or additives in TiO2 nanoparticles, such as metal/rare-earth cations and carbon materials, can engineer the interface matching with perovskite and improve the electron transport ability of TiO2 [15–19].

Carbon materials and derivatives, including graphene, graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes, have been widely developed as additives in PSCs to provide an efficient charge-collection channel and accelerate the interface electron extraction [20–24]. As a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been applied in PSCs due to their abundant functional groups on the surface, stable optical properties and high visible light transmittance [25–27]. It is clear that CDs can facilitate the electron extraction and transfer ability of electron transport layers. In addition, it is found that the effect of CDs on the electron transport would vary with their fluorescent and optical properties. Hui Wei et al. used red carbon dots doped SnO2 in a low-temperature solution as an electron transport layer, which improved the efficiency of PSCs from 19.15% to 22.77% and further enhanced their long-term moisture stability [28]. Li et al. reported a solution-treated carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/TiO2 composite in the planar heterojunction perovskite solar cells, which increased the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current density of the cell and the electron transport capacity of the electron transport layer [29].

In our previous study, self-synthesized TiO2 was proved to effectively replace the commercial dyesol-TiO2 as the electron transport layer of PSCs [30]. However, the charge transport performance of TiO2 is restricted by the surface defects. In this study, orange CDs prepared from p-phenylenediamine were employed to regulate the TiO2-involved electron transport. In order to illustrate the inner mechanism in PSCs, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), J-V curve, space charge limited current (SCLC) and photoluminescence (PL) were investigated. The experimental results showed that CDs not only improved electron mobility and charge extraction ability of the ETL, but also facilitated the growth of perovskite film. The introduction of appropriate content CDs can increase the efficiency of PSCs from 17.29% to 21.14%. Therefore, CDs have a broad application prospect in interface engineering and performance optimization of optoelectronic devices, which is worth further exploration.

CDs used in this experiment were synthesized by the solvothermal method. First, the p-phenylenediamine precursor was uniformly dissolved in DMF (DMF is the abbreviation of N, N-Dimethyl formamide) to form a transparent reddish solution. Then, the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with the above solution was heated in the oven at 200 ℃ for 4 h. After the reaction, the solution turned deep purple-red, and CDs were evenly dispersed in it [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles and pastes
ce:section.ce:para: Anatase TiO2 was synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method as the previous method [30]. Briefly, 2 mL of triethylamine (Aladdin, 99.0%) and a certain amount of tetra-butyl titanate (Aladdin, 99.0%) were slowly added to a beaker placed on a stirring table and filled with 60 mL of deionized water. After mixing well, it was then moved into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 200 ℃ for 24 h. A centrifuge and vacuum drying oven were applied to collect the white precipitate after the reaction. Self-made TiO2 nanoparticles and ethyl cellulose were dispersed in the mixture of terpineol and ethanol, and TiO2 paste was obtained after being evenly stirred. Before the device was fabricated, 1.0 g TiO2 paste was dispersed into 8.5 mL ethanol to prepare TiO2 dispersion solution for spin coating.

For the CDs modified TiO2, different amounts of CDs were dispersed into the TiO2 dispersion solution. The volume ratios of TiO2 dispersion solution and CDs solution were recorded as 5:1, 5:2 and 5:3, respectively. After homogenizing the mixed solution by ultrasonic, the TiO2-CDs dispersion solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of perovskite solar cells (FTO/Compact TiO2/TiO2-CDs/FAMA/Spiro/Ag)
ce:section.ce:para: The size of 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate needed to be continuously rinsed with detergent, deionized water, acetone and ethanol. Firstly, the titanium dioxide layer (c-TiO2) was spin coated on the FTO substrate. Next, the TiO2-CDs dispersion solution was rotated on the compact layer at a speed of 5000 rpm for 20 s, then sintered at 450 ℃ for 30 min, finally forming the TiO2 mesoporous layer (m-TiO2). After that, it was necessary to configure the core material of the light-absorbing layer, the perovskite precursor solution. It was synthesized by accurately weighing PbI2 (528.99 mg), PbBr2 (74.3 mg), FAI (formamidine hydrochloride, 187.96 mg) and MABr (methyl ammonium bromide, 22.65 mg), and then dissolving them in a mixture of DMF: DMSO (DMSO is the abbreviation of Dimethyl sulfoxide) with a volume ratio of 4:1. The perovskite precursor solution was deposited on the mesoporous layer in a rotating coating method (5000 RPM for 30 s), and then an appropriate amount of ethyl acetate was dropped on the rotating substrate and annealed at 100 ℃ for 30 min. The formation of perovskite structure is FA0.85MA0.15Pb(I0.85 Br0.15)3 (marked as FAMA). After it came down to room temperature, the Spiro-OMeTAD solution (18 µL Li-TFSI solution in acetonitrile, 72.3 mg Spiro-OMeTAD and 28.8 µL 4-tert-butylpyridine were successively added to 1 mL chlorobenzene) was spin-coated on the perovskite film at 4000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, at the pressure of 6 × 10−4 Pa, the Ag electrode with a thickness of 50 nm was thermally evaporated.

The data of sample morphological analysis came from field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, ZEISS-SIGMA HD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-F200, 200KV). The steady state photoluminescence (PL) of the film was acquired by using a FS5 integrated steady state transient fluorescence spectrometer, and the samples for the PL test were excited using a 390 nm laser. A solar energy source simulator (EASISOLAR-50–3A) was employed in generating AM 1.5 G (100 mW/cm2) illumination, and the photoelectric current-voltage (J-V) curve under the illumination was recorded by a digital light source meter (2400 source meter, Keithley Instruments Inc., USA). Then the photovoltaic characteristics of perovskite solar cells were analyzed using the effective data obtained. The incident photon-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the cells was measured with a Crown instrument. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and space charge limited current (SCLC) measurements were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (Bio-logic SAA SP-200).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs samples
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. S1 are the TEM images of self-synthesized TiO2 and TiO2-CDs. It could be observed that the morphology and structure of TiO2 nanoparticles have not changed after adding the CDs, with a particle size of about 20 nm. It is obviously shown in Fig. 1a that the particle size of orange CDs is generally about 5.5 nm, which are evenly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. Meanwhile, the crystal plane spacing of TiO2 remains 0.35 nm in the inverse Fourier transform map (Fig. 1a inset), corresponding to {101} crystal plane of anatase phase TiO2 [32,33]. For the chemical analysis of TiO2-CDs, the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping (Fig. 1b-f) show the distribution of O, C and Ti elements, and confirm that the tiny spherical particles observed in TEM images are indeed CDs. Fig. S2 shows the PL spectrum of the carbon dots, a strong peak in the 600 nm wavelength range, and the corresponding strong orange emission when excited by UV light of 365 nm are observed.

A series of TiO2-CDs photoanode with different content of CDs were applied in PSCs devices, volume ratios of TiO2 and CDs were recorded as 5:1, 5:2 and 5:3, respectively. The J-V curves of the PSCs are shown in Fig. 2a, and the detailed photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table 1, including open circuit voltage (Voc), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE). It can be seen that the efficiency of TiO2-based PSCs is about 17.3%, which is comparable to commercial Dyesol TiO2, verifying the reliability of the self-synthesized TiO2 layer used in the following experiment. Furthermore, it is evident that the PCE of PSCs based TiO2-CDs composite layer is significantly improved compared with the TiO2 layer.

As shown in Fig. 2a and Table 1, the highest PCE of PSCs is observed when the ratio of TiO2 and CDs is 5:2, with open circuit voltage (Voc) of 1.15 V, short circuit current (Jsc) of 26.04 mA/cm2, filling factor (FF) of 0.71 and photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of 21.14%. It is noteworthy that excessive CDs will introduce more recombination centers in the TiO2 layer, leading to the descent of efficiency.

The hysteresis effect of the PSCs based on control TiO2 and optimized TiO2-CDs (5:2) were investigated. By comparing the J-V curves in the forward and reverse scanning directions (in Fig. 2b), negligible hysteresis is observed for the TiO2-CDs-based PSCs, while the control TiO2-based PSCs still have a certain hysteresis effect. Meanwhile, the corresponding incident photon to the current efficiency (IPCE) of the two devices is compared in Fig. 2c. It is found that the device based TiO2-CDs exhibit higher incident photon to current efficiency in the wavelength range of 300–700 nm than that of the TiO2 device. The integral current densities calculated by the corresponding IPCE spectrum are 24.6 mA/cm2 and 23.4 mA/cm2 as shown on the right axis, which is consistent with the J-V result [34].

XRD and scanning electron microscopy images of FAMA perovskite film on TiO2 and TiO2-CDs layers are shown in Fig. 3. The peaks indexed to (001), (011), (111), (002), (012), (112), (022) and (003) diffraction peaks of the cubic phase FAMA are apparently shown for two samples. Perovskite films are composed of closely connected polygonal grains, while the size of polygonal grains on TiO2-CDs layer (Fig. 3c) is obviously larger than that of TiO2 (Fig. 3b). The large grain size can reduce the grain boundary, leading to the decreased charge recombination probability, thus enhancing the stability of the perovskite film [35].

The electron transport behavior of the TiO2/perovskite interface in perovskite solar cells was investigated by measuring steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) [36,37]. Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence spectrum of perovskite film on the different ETL (TiO2 or optimized TiO2-CDs). It is apparent that when the perovskite film is deposited on TiO2-CDs, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly, indicating that the electron transmission at the mesoporous layer/perovskite interface is improved. As shown in Fig. 4b, The TRPL results display a faster PL decay for the FAMA perovskite film deposited on TiO2-CDs than that of control TiO2. The shorter fluorescence lifetime (τ control TiO2 =6.4 ns τ TiO 2 /CDs=5.8 ns) indicates the faster electrons transfer from the perovskite to the mesoporous layer TiO2.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was further used to analyze the interface electron recombination at open circuit voltage under complete darkness [38]. Impedance spectrum fitted by Z-View and the equivalent circuit are shown in Fig. 4c. The low-frequency region on the right circle is represented by the electron recombination resistance (Rrec) between the perovskite layer and the TiO2 electron layer in PSCs. In the Nyquist diagram, the higher value of Rrec represents the larger charge recombination resistance at the interface. It can be clearly observed from Fig. 4c that the Rrec of TiO2-CDs-based PSCs is larger than that of TiO2-based PSCs, and the maximum Rrec (2.49 ×105 Ω) is obtained at the ratio of 5:2 (Fig. S3). The results indicate that the probability of electron recombination at the interface between the TiO2-CDs mesoporous layer and the perovskite layer is significantly reduced, leading to the efficient electron extraction.

Space charge limited current (SCLC) device with FTO/compact-TiO2 /TiO2-CDs/PCBM([6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric 6 acid methyl ester)/Ag structure was fabricated in Fig. S4 to explore the electron mobility of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs. The current-voltage characteristic curves of the devices were tested in a dark environment. The tested curves are divided into the Ohmic region, trap-filling limited (TFL) region and Child region [39,40]. Under the condition of low voltage (Ohmic region), the defect state is injected by the external electron, and the current is mainly generated by the drift of the electron inside the TiO2 under the electric field, which follows Ohm's law [41]. After all defect states are filled in TFL region, the injected electron will move freely within the TiO2 material. The voltage corresponding to the starting point of the TFL region is denoted as VTFL, which represents the voltage at which the defect state of TiO2 is completely filled. The corresponding defect density is shown in the following formula:
N
t
=
2 ε
ε
0
V
T F L
q
L
2
Where Nt is the defect density, ε is the dielectric constant of TiO2 material [42], ε0 is the vacuum dielectric constant, q is the basic charge; L is the thickness of the TiO2 layer (The thickness of the TiO2 layer in the PSCs came from section SEM, Fig. S5). When the injected electrons exceed the intrinsic electrons (Child region), the J-V properties change and space charges dominate. Therefore, in the Child region, the carrier mobility μ can be obtained by using the Mott-Gurney law through J-V relationship, as shown in the equation:
J =
9 8
μ
e
ε
ε
0
V
2
L
3
Where V is the bias voltage, J is the current density under the corresponding bias voltage, μe is electron mobility, ε is the dielectric constant of anatase phase TiO2, ε0 is vacuum dielectric constant, L is the thickness of TiO2 [43–45]. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S1, the minimum Nt and the largest electron mobility (μe) are obtained for the TiO2-CDs (5:2) sample. It suggests that an appropriate amount of CDs will reduce the defect state of TiO2 and increase the μe, but excessive CDs will lead to the introduction of the new defect state recombination center. The data of the control group (only TiO2) and the optimal TiO2-CDs are compared in Fig. 5, and the calculated parameters of the SCLC devices are listed in Table 2. It can be clearly seen from Table 2 that the Nt value of TiO2-CDs (5:2) has decreased from 2.16 × 1017 to 1.69 × 1017, the electron mobility (μe) value of TiO2-CDs (5:2) has increased from 3.00 × 10−5 to 4.13 × 10−5, indicating the positive effect of CDs on TiO2 electron transport capability.

Combined with the above analysis, it can be concluded that the addition of CDs can reduce the recombination probability of electron-hole at the interface and then promote the electron extraction efficiency from perovskite films. Meantime, the defect state density of TiO2 is restricted by CDs, resulting in the improved the mobility of photogenerated electrons in the TiO2 material, which ultimately enhances the performance of the PSCs device.

With the rapid development of photovoltaic technology in recent years, the stability of perovskite solar cells needs to be studied in parallel with the improvement of its efficiency. Therefore, corresponding tests were designed to detect the stability of PSCs [46,47].

The prepared devices were stored in the air with humidity of 30 ± 5% RH and temperature of 25 ± 2 ℃, then continuously tested their photovoltaic characteristics. The detailed parameters are illustrated in Fig. 6. The decay rate of the control TiO2-based PSCs is faster than that of the TiO2-CDs-based PSCs. Moreover, the performance parameter (PCE, Jsc, Voc, FF) of PSCs remained at above 85% of the initial value after 200 h storage, which showed better stability than the control TiO2 group. In addition, the statistic charts of photovoltaic characteristics for TiO2-CDs and control TiO2 are given in Fig. S7. The device based TiO2-CDs shows a smaller error range of FF, Voc, Jsc and PCE value than that of control TiO2.

As expected, enhanced interfacial electron transport and electron mobility in ETL for TiO2-CDs based devices can lead to an improvement of the stability and repeatability. The above studies confirm that the TiO2-CDs can be used as an efficient ETL layer for high-efficiency PSCs.

In summary, the role of CDs on TiO2-involved electron transport is mainly reflected in reducing interface electron recombination, improving the electron extraction ability from perovskite to TiO2, and enhancing the electron mobility of TiO2. The photoelectric conversion efficiency reached 21.14% when TiO2-CDs were used as ETL, which is higher than that of TiO2-based PSCs (17.29%) under the same conditions. Furthermore, the TiO2-CDs based PSCs display negligible hysteresis and improved stability and repeatability. This work elaborated on the effect of CDs on the internal mechanism of electron transfer involving TiO2 in PSCs.

Bingchen Huo: Investigation, Methodology, Writing − original draft. Suping Jia: Project administration, Writing − review & editing, Resources. Yangyang Li: Investigation, Methodology. Huinian Zhang: Data curation. Caihong Hao: Data curation. Huiqi Wang: Formal analysis. Shengliang Hu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science Foundation of North University of China (XJJ201913), the Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2019L0597, 2020L0293), and the fund from Key Laboratory of Hubei Province for Coal Conversion and New Carbon Materials (Wuhan University of Science and Technology) (WKDM202202).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169958.

ce:section.ce:para:
